era_context: The Western Roman Empire is fragmenting under military pressure and political instability. Alaric I and the Visigoths are operating in the Balkans and threatening Italy. The Rhine frontier has collapsed, with Germanic tribes (Vandals, Alans, Sueves, Franks) crossing into Gaul. In Britain, Roman military authority is weakening as troops are withdrawn to defend the continent. The usurper Constantine III has just been proclaimed emperor by troops in Britain. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius remains more stable but is increasingly independent of the West. This is the era of the 'barbarian invasions' and the fragmentation of imperial authority.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine (sparse documentation)
East Asia (contemporary Chinese sources may exist but are underrepresented)

events.title: Constantine III proclaimed emperor in Britain
events.description: In 407, Roman troops in Britain, facing pressure from Picts and other northern tribes and dissatisfied with the central government's inability to defend the province, proclaimed Constantine, a Roman general, as emperor. Constantine III, as he became known, quickly moved to Gaul and established his capital at Arles. He claimed legitimate imperial authority and minted coins in his name. Constantine III would control much of Gaul and Hispania for several years, representing a major fragmentation of imperial authority. His regime would eventually be defeated in 411, but his existence highlighted the Western Roman Empire's inability to maintain unified control.
key_figures: Gerontius (Roman general, initially supporting Constantine III)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Numismatic evidence: coins minted in Constantine III's name

events.certainty_note: Constantine III's proclamation in 407 is attested in multiple sources and confirmed by numismatic evidence. The date is well established.
cross_references: Led to 408-003 (Constantine III's consolidation of power)

events.title: Rhine frontier collapses; Germanic tribes cross into Gaul
events.description: In 407 (or possibly 406), the Rhine frontier, which had been the boundary of the Roman Empire for centuries, collapsed. Germanic tribes—Vandals, Alans, Sueves, and Franks—crossed the frozen Rhine and entered Gaul. This was not a sudden military conquest but rather a migration of entire peoples seeking new territory, possibly driven by pressure from the Huns in Central Asia. The crossing marked the beginning of the end of Roman Gaul and the establishment of Germanic kingdoms in the region. The exact date is debated (406 or 407), but 407 is conventional. The crossing was facilitated by the withdrawal of Roman troops to defend Italy against Alaric.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Archaeological evidence: settlement patterns, material culture

events.certainty_note: The crossing of Germanic tribes into Gaul is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is debated. Zosimus and Orosius place it in 406 or 407; Hydatius places it in 406. The date 407 is conventional but not certain. The scale and nature of the crossing are also debated—it may have been a gradual migration rather than a sudden invasion.
cross_references: Part of broader Germanic migration into Roman territory

events.title: Alaric I and the Visigoths threaten Italy
events.description: In 407, Alaric I, king of the Visigoths, was operating in the Balkans and threatening to march on Italy. Alaric had been seeking land and recognition from the Roman Empire for over a decade and had been in conflict with Roman generals, particularly Stilicho. The threat of Alaric's invasion prompted the withdrawal of Roman troops from the Rhine frontier and Britain to defend Italy. Alaric's pressure on Italy would culminate in his march on Rome in 408 and the sack of the city in 410.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Alaric's presence in the Balkans and his threat to Italy are attested in sources, but the precise chronology is uncertain. Zosimus and Orosius are writing decades later.

events.title: Arcadius dies; Theodosius II becomes Eastern Roman Emperor
events.description: In 408 (not 407, but included here for chronological context), Arcadius, Eastern Roman Emperor, died. His son Theodosius II, age seven, succeeded him. Theodosius II would reign for 42 years and would be responsible for major legal and administrative reforms. However, the succession occurred in 408, not 407. This entry is included to note that by 407, Arcadius was aging and the succession was imminent.
events.certainty_note: Arcadius's death in 408 is well documented. This event is included for chronological context but occurred in 408, not 407.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the Rhine crossing in 406/407 as a sudden 'invasion' has been significantly revised by modern scholarship. Rather than a military conquest, the crossing may have been a gradual migration process, possibly facilitated by Roman authorities seeking military allies. The scale of the crossing is also debated—it may have involved far fewer people than traditional sources suggest. Additionally, the degree of disruption to Roman Gaul is uncertain; some regions may have continued functioning under Roman or Romano-Germanic administration for decades after 406/407. The traditional narrative of 'invasion and conquest' obscures the complexity of late antique settlement and integration. Furthermore, the date of the Rhine crossing is debated between 406 and 407; some sources place it in 406, others in 407. The conventional date of 406 is now more widely accepted by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The year 407 is moderately well documented for events in the Mediterranean and Italy, but sources are unevenly distributed. Zosimus and Orosius are the primary sources for Alaric's movements and Constantine III's proclamation, but both are writing decades later. Contemporary written records are sparse; most information comes from later compilations and chronicles. Archaeological evidence (coins, pottery, settlement patterns) provides some corroboration but is unevenly distributed. Events in Britain, Gaul, northern Europe, and beyond the Roman frontier are less thoroughly documented. The sources are heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Roman perspective. The exact date of the Rhine crossing is debated, with some sources placing it in 406 rather than 407.

graph_edges.note: Constantine III's proclamation in 407 led to his consolidation of power in Gaul and Hispania in 408.
The Rhine crossing in 407 led to the crossing into Hispania in 409.
Alaric's threat to Italy in 407 led to his march on Rome in 408.
